stem: Let \( a = [[a]] \) and \( b = [[b]] \). Using the identity \( \gcd(a,b)\cdot\operatorname{lcm}(a,b) = ab \), what is the value of \( \gcd(a,b)\cdot\operatorname{lcm}(a,b) \)?
solution: The product \( \gcd(a,b)\cdot\operatorname{lcm}(a,b) \) always equals \( ab \). Hence the value is \( [[a]]\cdot[[b]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( a + b \); the identity involves the product \( ab \), not the sum.
This is \( \operatorname{lcm}(a,b) \) alone, forgetting the factor of \( \gcd(a,b) \).
This multiplies the gcd by the sum instead of forming the product \( ab \).
This overshoots \( ab \) by one gcd.
This undershoots \( ab \) by one gcd.
This is two more than the correct product.
This is two less than the correct product.

stem: Two positive integers \( a \) and \( b \) have \( \gcd(a,b) = [[G]] \) and \( \operatorname{lcm}(a,b) = [[L]] \). If \( a = [[a]] \), what is \( b \)?
distractor_rationales: This forgets to multiply by \( \gcd(a,b) \) before dividing by \( a \).
This just returns \( a \) itself, as if \( a=b \).
This subtracts instead of using the product-quotient relation.
This divides by \( \gcd(a,b) \) instead of by \( a \).
This overshoots the correct value by \( \gcd(a,b) \).
This undershoots the correct value by \( \gcd(a,b) \).
This is one more than the correct value.

stem: Two rotating gears in a machine each complete a whole number of cycles per minute. The product of their two cycle-lengths (in seconds) is \( [[P]] \), and the greatest common divisor of the two cycle-lengths is \( [[G]] \). What is the least common multiple of the two cycle-lengths, i.e. after how many seconds will both gears next complete a cycle at exactly the same moment?
solution: Since \( \gcd \cdot \operatorname{lcm} = \text{product} \) for any two positive integers, the lcm equals \( \dfrac{P}{\gcd} = \dfrac{[[P]]}{[[G]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies by the gcd instead of dividing by it.
This is just the product itself, forgetting to divide out the gcd at all.
This is just the gcd itself, not the lcm.
This divides by the gcd squared instead of the gcd.
This overshoots the lcm by one gcd.
This undershoots the lcm by one gcd.
This is one more than the correct lcm.
